column1: How do users experience friction, support, and self-service breakdowns in the product journey?
self-service paths blocked by functional failures led to task abandonment or support escalation
column3: Directly describes self-service breakdown: specific tasks fail because UI mechanisms do not work; customers abandon or escalate to support

column1: navigation and findability friction increased effort to access features or complete tasks
column3: Directly describes friction and self-service breakdown: features cannot be reached; self-service requires significantly more effort than expected

column1: unexplained or unannounced system failures created uncertainty for customers
column3: Describes a distinct type of friction (reliability + communication gap) relevant to the research question but represents a single below-threshold candidate with insufficient evidence to form a broader theme alone

column1: valued features or improvements have remaining capability or performance gaps
column3: Constructive and comparative commentary; does not describe friction or self-service breakdown as defined by the research question

column1: self-service breakdown: customers were blocked from completing tasks independently by broken mechanisms or navigation friction
column2: A pattern in which customers attempted product tasks without assistance but could not complete them — either because a specific UI mechanism failed or because the route to the feature could not be found — resulting in task abandonment, significantly increased effort, or forced escalation to support.
column3: self-service paths blocked by functional failures led to task abandonment or support escalation; navigation and findability friction increased effort to access features or complete tasks
column4: irrelevant search results caused abandonment of self-service and escalation to support; unresponsive upgrade button led to task abandonment; broken cancellation page forced support escalation after repeated self-service failure; billing navigation failure blocked self-service task; buried features and low intuitiveness increase effort to find functionality
column6: Both theme candidates describe the same broader outcome — self-service failure — through different proximate mechanisms. TC1 involves UI mechanisms that were reached but did not function; TC2 involves features that could not be reached due to navigation or findability barriers. Under the research question's focus on self-service breakdowns, both represent the same failure: the customer attempted independent task completion and could not succeed. The two sub-patterns are analytically distinct and must be preserved within this broader theme — they point to different remediation paths (fix broken mechanisms vs improve navigation) even though they share the same outcome and the same impact (task abandonment or increased effort).
column8: TC2 (navigation and findability friction) was below the 3-response threshold in Stage 2 (2 responses). Grouping it with TC1 under this broader theme does not resolve that concern — the underlying evidence base for TC2 remains thin. Analyst should consider whether to treat TC2 as a distinct sub-theme pending more data, or whether TC1 alone is sufficient as a High-confidence standalone broader theme. TC1 alone would yield: High confidence, R002 R004 R008, three responses, all High confidence source rows. Additionally, R007 (mobile platform dysfunction) and R012 (onboarding near-abandonment) from Stage 2 isolated codes are directly relevant to this broader theme and could strengthen it in a larger dataset.

column1: unexplained or unannounced system failures created uncertainty for customers
column2: unexpected logout caused form data loss during active task; repeated integration failures without communication eroded reliability expectations
column4: Single theme candidate; Low confidence at Stage 2; below the 3-response threshold. A broader theme based on one candidate must be marked Low or moved to peripheral. While this candidate is peripherally relevant to the research question (reliability friction, absence of communication as a compounding failure), the evidence is insufficient to support a standalone broader theme. Grouping with BT1 was considered but rejected — the defining element here is absence of communication/explanation, which is distinct from the functional failure or navigation mechanisms in BT1.
column5: Consider as a distinct singleton finding on reliability and communication gaps. Revisit with more data. Analyst may also consider whether this candidate connects with R007 (mobile dysfunction) and R011 (non-functional preference controls) from Stage 2 isolated codes — all involve system behavior the customer cannot explain or control. If that connection holds in a larger dataset, a second broader theme may be warranted.

column1: valued features or improvements have remaining capability or performance gaps
column2: dashboard improvement reveals performance gap in reports section; export feature valued but absence of scheduling creates recurring manual work
column4: Single theme candidate; Low confidence at Stage 2; below the 3-response threshold. Out of scope for the current research question — this candidate describes constructive and comparative commentary (things that partially work) rather than friction, support breakdown, or self-service failure as defined by the research question. The research question is an analytic lens that has placed this candidate outside the current scope.
column5: Treat as capability/roadmap input rather than a friction or self-service finding. This candidate may be central under a different research question (e.g., 'How well does the product meet user needs for automation and consistent performance?'). Document as a peripheral finding and surface to product/roadmap stakeholders separately.

column1: STAGE 2 ISOLATED CODES — NOTED AS CONTEXT

column2: option overload at onboarding nearly caused first-day abandonment
column3: High — directly relevant to self-service breakdown
column4: Onboarding is the first self-service task in the product journey. Near-abandonment at this stage is analytically significant and directly relevant to the research question. Single response — insufficient for a theme candidate — but worth noting as a singleton finding on self-service entry failure.

column2: non-functional buttons and broken layout blocked mobile use
column3: High — directly relevant to self-service breakdown via mobile channel
column4: Mobile is a self-service channel. A broadly broken mobile experience prevents any self-service task completion on that platform. Single response. Could extend BT1 if validated in more data — the mechanism (non-functional UI) matches TC1's pattern.

column2: transaction history limit blocked access needed for a stated purpose
column3: Peripheral — a capability barrier to self-service
column4: A data access limit preventing a customer from retrieving their own records is a form of self-service friction. Single response. Not included in BT1 because the mechanism (product scope limit) is different from functional failure or navigation friction.

column2: non-functional preference controls undermine notification relevance
column4: Setting preferences is a form of self-service (user controls their own experience). When those controls appear non-functional, the self-service expectation fails. Single response. May connect with TC3 (system behavior the customer cannot explain).

column2: guided onboarding resources supported successful self-service setup
column4: Directly relevant as a contrasting self-service success. Suggests that when onboarding resources are present and well-structured, self-service can succeed. Provides a useful counterpoint to R012 and the broader self-service breakdown theme.

column2: fast support response met resolution expectations
column3: Peripheral — relevant to the support dimension of the research question
column4: When self-service fails, support is the recovery channel. This response shows that fast support resolution meets expectations. Provides context for the support dimension of the research question — support CAN work well even when self-service does not.
